summary: McKenzie describes feeling the entrepreneurial urge to build something new after a period of focusing on his day job. A coworker's simple question about why he doesn't just build something forces him to confront his own inertia. He acknowledges he has the funds, knowledge, and capability but lacks time due to his demanding job. He's considering a web application that can be built in short sprints, and mentions a recurring dream about an application with a terrible business model.
key_points: The entrepreneurial itch often starts from something tangential — in this case, admiring icon sets triggered the desire to build
Having resources (money, knowledge, experience) doesn't automatically overcome inertia — sometimes it takes someone else asking the obvious question
Practical constraints shape product decisions: limited time after midnight shifts points toward web apps over desktop software
McKenzie recognizes the tension between building something he'd love to use versus something with a viable business model
money_quotes: Every time I see a good, pretty icon set I get the urge to make an application just to be able to use them.
I certainly am not hurting for startup funds this time around, and I know what needs to be done to get a business up and running.
I have also been having persistent dreams of a particular application. They've gotten so vivid as to include database schema…
The waking me knows that the business model is terrible. Just asking for total failure. Nobody could ever pay money for it.
It will have to be something where I can see progress with a bunch of 2 hour mini-sprints after getting in the door around midnight.
key_insight: The gap between knowing you should build something and actually starting is often not resources or knowledge but simple inertia, easily broken by someone asking the right question.
